inline_comments.message: Aren't these for python3?
Aren't these for Docker?

Will it be interpreted as float when parsed? (I'm not fluent in YAML)

(6 more occurrences in this file)
Quoting strings was dropped from all notes but this one - how is it different from others?

Float:

>>> with open('recommended_versions.yaml', 'r') as f:
...  yml = f.read()
>>> type(yaml.load(yml)['Kubernetes']['recommended_version'])
<class 'float'>

I guess it could be put in quotes but this really depends on the tool that is reading this file - if it does type casting itself then this is not an issue.

I gave a try with pyyaml

>>> import yaml
>>> with open('recommended_versions.yaml') as f:
    
    data = yaml.load(f, Loader=yaml.FullLoader)
    print(data)
 
{'java11': {'recommended_version': 'Java SE 11.0.6', 'release_date': datetime.date(2020, 1, 14), 'notes': 'Based on the Java 11 release history, there will likely be  new releases in April, July and November.\n'}, 'python3': {'recommended_version': '3.8.3', 'release_date': datetime.date(2019, 10, 14), 'notes': 'Next release is planned for 2020-10-05'}, 'Docker': {'recommended_version': '19.03.8', 'release_date': datetime.date(2020, 3, 10)}, 'Kubernetes': {'recommended_version': 1.17, 'release_date': datetime.date(2019, 12, 9), 'notes': '1.18.0-rc.1 was released on 2020-03-17. It is not clear if memcached is still supported by the community\n'}, 'netbox': {'recommended_version': '2.8.1', 'release_date': datetime.date(2020, 4, 23), 'notes': 'in contrib'}, 'awx': {'recommended_version': '11.2.0', 'release_date': datetime.date(2020, 4, 29), 'notes': 'in contrib'}, 'ejbca': {'recommended_version': '6.15.2.6', 'notes': 'in contrib'}, 'rabbitmq': {'recommended_version': ['3.7.25', '3.8.3'], 'release_date': [datetime.date(2020, 4, 9), datetime.date(2020, 3, 10)], 'notes': 'used by multicloud fcaps.3.7.25 is the final release in the 3.7.x series and GitHub recommends using 3.8.3\n'}}
>>> type(data)
<class 'dict'>
>>> data['java11']
{'recommended_version': 'Java SE 11.0.6', 'release_date': datetime.date(2020, 1, 14), 'notes': 'Based on the Java 11 release history, there will likely be  new releases in April, July and November.\n'}
>>> data['Kafka']
{'recommended_version': '2.4.1', 'release_date': datetime.date(2020, 3, 12)}
>>> data['consul']
{'recommended_version': ['1.6.5', '1.7.2'], 'release_date': [datetime.date(2020, 4, 13), datetime.date(2020, 3, 16)], 'notes': 'in AAF SMS + in consul'}
>>> data['Kafka']['recommended_version']
'2.4.1'
>>> type(data['Kafka']['recommended_version'])
<class 'str'>

it is string
my bad
Why Java SE 11.0.6 instead of just 11.0.6?
Stupid newbie question... Why do we need release_date? Do we care about it? Version should be enough to identify uniquely package...

I'm not a fan of two lists.
If we really need this release data information I'd prefer to put make this a list of objects and put both fields in a single object like:

 versions:
   - version: 1.4.1
     date: 2020-04-30
   - version: 1.3.5
     date: 2020-04-20

Personally I consider this to be more yaml-native;)
Ack

I copied/pasted the SECCOM wiki page...
I am not sure to see the need for the release date, looks redundant with version indication
I can remove it if you agree

you are right
I will do it this way if it is confirmed that the release date is needed
I recommend that we have ONAP delivered with the TLS 1.2 and 1.3 Intermediate ciphers enabled. See https://wiki.mozilla.org/Security/Server_Side_TLS#Cipher_names_correspondence_table.

I recommend using the default ciphers that are enabled by OpenSSH. See https://man.openbsd.org/sshd_config, ciphers. The default ciphers are
chacha20-poly1305@openssh.com,
aes128-ctr,aes192-ctr,aes256-ctr,
aes128-gcm@openssh.com,aes256-gcm@openssh.com
When we put the list together we opted for Java SE.

The list was taken from the wiki (https://wiki.onap.org/display/DW/Database%2C+Java%2C+Python%2C+Docker%2C+Kubernetes%2C+and+Image+Versions). We included a date as information. The list is one the wiki to provide easy access and to get feedback from the community.

Suggested change to the comment:
This file lists the SECCOM recommended versions of various components for the Guilin release

as it is versioned, I do not think it is needed.
As it is introduced now, it is for master (Guilin)
once Guilin will be there will be create a branch and you could update on master for the next releases.

As a conclusion thanks to gerrit branch, it will always be possible to know to which version it corresponds
+1 to Morgan rationale
